response_raw:
## 1. Rider objective & context

The footage appears to be a first-person (helmet- or chest-mounted) POV recording of an off-road motorcycle ride through a woodland trail network. The camera is a DJI Osmo Action 5 Pro, timestamped 2026. The rider is navigating through a deciduous forest on what appears to be a network of dirt trails and open terrain sections.

---

## 2. Rider

The rider is not directly visible in any frame. Only the motorcycle's cockpit area (handlebars, top clamp, front number plate/shroud area) is visible in the POV frames. Renthal-branded handlebar pad/bar is visible. A gloved hand is briefly visible in one late frame (pink/magenta glove). No body position, helmet, or protective gear is otherwise observable.

---

## 3. Terrain & environment

- Deciduous forest with predominantly bare trees, consistent with late autumn or winter conditions
- Ground covered heavily in dry fallen leaves, with visible dirt, rock outcroppings, and exposed roots
- Trail transitions from a wider, more defined dirt path with eroded banks (early frames) to progressively narrower, less defined single-track threading between trees
- Terrain is hilly with visible elevation changes; slopes rise on both sides of the trail in several frames
- Fallen and downed branches/small trees are visible across the trail in later frames
- Sky is overcast/grey throughout; no precipitation visible
- Frost-like appearance on grass in some mid-sequence frames, suggesting cold conditions
- Some green vegetation (evergreen shrubs) visible in isolated frames

---

## 4. Events

- **Frames 1–2:** Wide dirt trail visible from what appears to be a stationary or slow-moving vantage point; no motorcycle visible yet in-frame.
- **Frame 3:** POV perspective establishes — motorcycle handlebars and red front shroud of the bike come into frame; rider is moving along a narrowing trail between embankments.
- **Frames 4–6:** The motorcycle continues forward through progressively narrower, more overgrown terrain; the camera angle tilts, suggesting movement over uneven ground.
- **Frame 7 (closeup of Renthal bar pad):** The camera angle drops sharply and tilts heavily — the Renthal handlebar pad is prominently in frame at a skewed angle, consistent with the camera/bike orientation changing significantly (possible fall beginning).
- **Frame 8:** The camera angle is now steeply angled upward toward the trees; the motorcycle's front end and handlebars are low in frame and appear to be at an abnormal lateral tilt.
- **Frames 9–10:** The bike appears to be leaning heavily or on its side; the camera is pointed laterally/upward rather than forward; small branches are visible in the foreground.
- **Frame 11:** The motorcycle is clearly on its side on the ground — the rear wheel, frame, red bodywork (marked "GASGAS"), and fork are all horizontal; multiple downed branches surround the bike.
- **Frame 12:** Similar view — motorcycle lying on its side, front wheel and fork visible; more downed branch debris visible across the hillside.
- **Frame 13 (close-up):** Ground-level close-up of the fallen motorcycle. The red GASGAS-branded bodywork is clearly readable (reversed, as camera is now inverted or ground-level). The Renthal handlebar is visible. The bodywork shows extensive pre-existing scratching and wear. A gloved hand (pink/magenta) is partially visible at the bottom right of frame, appearing to reach toward the bike.

---

## 5. Outcome

The motorcycle is observed lying on its side on a leaf-covered, sloped hillside among downed branches. The final frames show the bike stationary on the ground. A gloved hand is visible in the last frame reaching toward the bike or camera. The motorcycle (identified as a GASGAS brand) is visibly on the ground and not upright.

---

## 6. Unclear or not visible

- The rider's body is not visible at any point; it is unknown whether the rider remained with the bike or separated from it
- No rider injury or condition is visible or determinable
- The exact moment or cause of the bike going to the ground is not captured between frames due to the interval-based extraction
- Speed at any point is not determinable
- Whether the engine remained running after the bike went down is not visible
- The specific section of trail or geographic location is not identifiable
- Whether any downed branches were struck prior to the bike going down is unclear
- The rider's protective gear (beyond one gloved hand) is not visible
